JuliaActuary

  • Learn

One Abstraction for Yields, Mortality, Lapse, and Defaults

JuliaActuary is an ecosystem of packages that makes Julia the easiest language to get started for actuarial workflows.
code
blog
Author

Alec Loudenback

Published

May 4, 2026

Actuaries already think about the same algebra under four different names. The discount factor on a yield curve is a multiplicative scaling we apply to a future cashflow. So is the survival probability on a mortality table. So is persistency on a lapse curve. So is no-default probability on a credit spread. When we price a contingent annuity we write something like

\[ EPV = \sum_t c_t \cdot v(0,t) \cdot \,_tp_x \cdot \,_tp_x^{(\ell)} \]

— and the only reason v, p, and p^(ℓ) look different in the code is that each lives in a different package with its own API. Math says they’re the same shape; the codebase didn’t.

A coordinated four-package release across the JuliaActuary ecosystem fixes that. AbstractDeflator in FinanceCore.jl 2.6.0 is the unifying abstract type, with one required primitive (factor(p, from, to)) and one composition operator (compose(...)). FinanceModels.jl 5.4.0 brings every yield curve under it. MortalityTables.jl 2.7.0 does the same for parametric mortality and adds a MortalityVector wrapper for table-based mortality. ActuaryUtilities.jl 5.6.0 picks up the new compat. The result: pv(compose(yield_curve, mortality_table, lapse, default_force), cashflows) is one expression, and the same compose and pv calls work whether the components are constant forces, fitted yield curves, parametric mortality models, or q_x tables.

This post walks through the abstraction and shows it working across the ecosystem: a real term-structure yield curve composed with mortality for a contingent annuity, defaultable bond pricing with ELGD-of-market-value, sensitivity analysis by component swap, and the joint-life and last-survivor patterns that drop out of the abstraction or extend it cleanly.

The abstraction

AbstractDeflator is a supertype for processes that act as a multiplicative factor over time. Every concrete subtype implements one method:

factor(p::AbstractDeflator, from, to)

That’s the multiplicative factor over [from, to]. The interpretation depends on the subtype:

  • For yield curves and constant-force Rates, factor is the discount factor.
  • For mortality, lapse, and default processes, factor is the survival probability.
  • For asset accumulation paths and equity scenarios, factor is the accumulation factor S(to)/S(from).

There’s also an optional method:

intensity(p::AbstractDeflator, t)

— the instantaneous force at time t, defined as -d/dt log(factor(p, 0, t)) where the derivative exists. Continuous-time subtypes implement this; discrete tables (annual q_x, rating-transition matrices) deliberately don’t, and asking for intensity on them raises a MethodError. The type system surfaces the continuity assumption.

Once a subtype provides factor, it gets discount, accumulation, forward, and the actuarial-overload Base.zero (spot rate) for free:

discount(p, from, to)     # = factor(p, from, to)
accumulation(p, from, to) # = inv(factor(p, from, to))
forward(p, from, to)      # = Continuous(-log(factor(p, from, to)) / (to - from))
zero(p, t)                # = Continuous(-log(factor(p, t)) / t)  -- spot rate

The Base.zero(p, t) form is an actuarial overload: in the FinanceCore convention, this returns the continuously-compounded zero rate (the constant continuous force that, applied uniformly over [0, t], would produce the same factor). It’s not the additive-identity meaning of zero(x) — the two-argument form disambiguates by signature.

Composition: compose

The point of having one abstract type is being able to combine instances. compose(args...) builds a CompositeDeflator whose factor is the product of the components’ factors:

julia> using FinanceCore

julia> i = Continuous(0.03);    # 3% interest force

julia> μ = Continuous(0.012);   # 1.2% mortality force

julia> deflator = compose(i, μ)
compose(Continuous(0.03), Continuous(0.012))

julia> discount(deflator, 5)
0.8105842459701871

julia> exp(-(0.03 + 0.012) * 5)
0.8105842459701871

The composite’s intensity is the sum of component intensities (chain rule on log(factor) = Σ log(factor_i)):

julia> intensity(deflator, 1.0)
0.041999999999999996

And it composes with the existing pv machinery on Cashflow vectors:

julia> pv(deflator, [Cashflow(1.0, 5.0)])
0.8105842459701871

That’s the EPV of $1 paid in 5 years contingent on survival, using a single deterministic 1.2% mortality force.

compose flattens nested composites, so left-associated and right-associated builds produce the same shape. Three-decrement composites read naturally:

julia> i   = Continuous(0.05);

julia> μ   = Continuous(0.01);   # mortality

julia> λ_l = Continuous(0.07);   # lapse

julia> λ_d = Continuous(0.001);  # default

julia> d = compose(i, μ, λ_l, λ_d);

julia> intensity(d, 0)           # sum of forces
0.131

Composition assumes the components are independent. The structural product is the right shape for the 95% case in actuarial work (interest is independent of mortality is independent of lapse is independent of default, in the textbook formulation). When that assumption breaks — joint mortalities under a copula, common-shock defaults, correlated yield-and-default scenarios — you write a bespoke AbstractDeflator subtype whose factor is not a product of marginals. We’ll see this pattern below.

Use case 1: Term-structure yield curve × mortality table

The headline workflow now spans four packages. Take a real-ish US Treasury–style yield curve, fit a cubic spline to it, and compose with a mortality vector aligned to the valuation age via at_age:

julia> using FinanceCore, FinanceModels, MortalityTables

julia> # Fitted yield curve, multiple maturities
julia> mats  = [0.5, 1.0, 2.0, 3.0, 5.0, 7.0, 10.0, 20.0, 30.0];

julia> rates = [0.045, 0.046, 0.043, 0.041, 0.040, 0.041, 0.042, 0.046, 0.048];

julia> yc = fit(Spline.Cubic(), CMTYield.(rates, mats), Fit.Bootstrap());

julia> yc isa AbstractDeflator     # FinanceModels yields are deflators now
true

julia> # Mortality vector indexed by attained age
julia> qs = UltimateMortality([min(0.0001 * 1.097^age, 1.0) for age in 0:119])
MortalityVector(<120 rates, ages 0:119>)

julia> qs isa AbstractDeflator     # MortalityTables 3.0 returns a MortalityVector
true

julia> # Align to a 65-year-old's valuation date
julia> mort = at_age(qs, 65)
at_age(MortalityVector(<120 rates, ages 0:119>), 65)

julia> # Compose: yield × mortality on a years-from-valuation axis
julia> deflator = compose(yc, mort);

julia> # 20-year temporary life annuity-immediate, $50,000/year
julia> cashflows = [Cashflow(50_000.0, t) for t in 1:20];

julia> pv(deflator, cashflows)        # contingent EPV
374443.96...

julia> pv(yc, cashflows)              # annuity-certain (no mortality)
654791.10...

The mortality contingency cuts the value of a 65-year-old’s 20-year annuity-immediate from $654,791 to $374,444 — about a 43% reduction reflecting the cumulative survival probabilities through age 85 against this synthetic table.

The at_age wrapper is the key axis-alignment step. The yield curve uses a years-from-valuation axis (t = 0 is today). The raw mortality vector uses an absolute-age axis (index 65 is q_65). Composing them directly would silently mix axes — the yield’s factor(yc, 5) would be the spot 5y discount, but the mortality’s factor(qs, 5) would be “survival from age 0 to age 5.” at_age(qs, 65) shifts the mortality’s origin so that factor(mort, t) means “5-year survival from age 65” — same axis as the yield. This is the forward-vs-spot footgun the abstraction’s documentation warns about; at_age is the canonical fix and works for both MortalityVector and ParametricMortality (Makeham, Gompertz, etc.).

Use case 2: Defaultable bond pricing with ELGD

Reduced-form credit modeling under the recovery-of-market-value convention (Duffie-Singleton 1999) prices a defaultable bond as

\[ \bar{P}(0,T) = E\left[\exp\left(-\int_0^T \big(r(s) + \mathrm{ELGD}\cdot\lambda(s)\big)\,ds\right)\right] \]

where ELGD = 1 − R is the loss given default. This is the dominant modern convention in CDS-implied modeling and Solvency II spread risk. The recipe in code is one line — the existing Rate * Real operator already scales the underlying force:

julia> # Same fitted yield curve as above
julia> bond_cfs = [Cashflow(t == 5.0 ? 102.0 : 2.0, t) for t in 0.5:0.5:5.0];

julia> # 4% annual coupon, semi-annual, par = 100

julia> λ_marginal = Continuous(0.012);          # 120bp marginal default hazard

julia> ELGD       = 0.60;                        # 60% loss given default

julia> λ_loss_adj = λ_marginal * ELGD;           # Continuous(0.0072) — Rate * Real

julia> pv(yc, bond_cfs)                          # risk-free price
100.0015...

julia> pv(compose(yc, λ_marginal), bond_cfs)     # zero-recovery defaultable
94.6585...

julia> pv(compose(yc, λ_loss_adj), bond_cfs)     # 40% recovery (ELGD = 60%)
96.7586...

The implied credit spread maps cleanly onto the loss-adjusted hazard:

julia> rate(forward(compose(yc, λ_marginal), 0, 5)) - rate(forward(yc, 0, 5))
0.012   # 120bp = 100% LGD × 120bp hazard

julia> rate(forward(compose(yc, λ_loss_adj), 0, 5)) - rate(forward(yc, 0, 5))
0.0072  # 72bp = 60% LGD × 120bp hazard

The pattern generalizes. Time-varying ELGD or stochastic ELGD correlated with the default intensity (Frye 2000) doesn’t fit Continuous(ELGD * λ) directly — but it fits as a custom AbstractDeflator subtype that defines its own factor integrating (1-R(t)) · λ(t) over the interval. The framework provides the slot; the credit modeler provides the math.

Use case 3: Analytical sensitivities via ForwardDiff

A common actuarial workflow is “what’s the reserve sensitive to?” The traditional finite-difference approach computes the reserve at the base assumption, computes it again at a shocked assumption, and takes the ratio. That works, but it commits to a specific shock magnitude up front and accumulates floating-point error from the differencing.

The deflator abstraction is autodiff-friendly by construction. factor, compose, and pv are all type-generic — Continuous(μ::ForwardDiff.Dual) produces a dual-typed Rate, the compose machinery preserves it through the product, and pv carries it all the way to the scalar. So one ForwardDiff.derivative call gives you the analytical sensitivity:

julia> using ForwardDiff

julia> yield     = Continuous(0.03);

julia> cashflows = [Cashflow(100.0, t) for t in 1.0:10.0];

julia> # PV as a function of the mortality force μ
julia> pv_of_μ(μ) = pv(compose(yield, Continuous(μ)), cashflows);

julia> base_pv = pv_of_μ(0.012)
799.5276...

julia> # Analytical d(PV)/dμ — exact, no shock magnitude required
julia> dpv_dμ = ForwardDiff.derivative(pv_of_μ, 0.012)
-4121.1845...

julia> # Mortality semi-elasticity: -d(log PV) / dμ
julia> -dpv_dμ / base_pv
5.1545...

julia> # Convert to the "10% relative mortality shock" framing if you want it:
julia> # Δμ = 0.10 × 0.012 = 0.0012, so linear-approx impact is dPV/dμ × Δμ
julia> dpv_dμ * 0.0012 / base_pv
-0.00619...

The ForwardDiff path produces dPV/dμ = −4,121.18 exactly. Translated to the original “10% shock” framing via the linear approximation, that’s a −0.62% change in PV — within rounding of the −0.62% you’d get from the finite-difference computation, but without committing to a shock magnitude. You compute the sensitivity once and apply it to whatever shock size your stress framework uses.

The same pipeline gives you the sensitivity to each element of the composed deflator in one AD pass. Pair each component of compose(...) with its own parameter and call ForwardDiff.gradient:

julia> # 4-decrement deflator: interest × mortality × lapse × default,
julia> # one parameter per element of the composite
julia> function pv_of_components(i, μ, λ_l, λ_d)
           deflator = compose(Continuous(i),
                              Continuous(μ),
                              Continuous(λ_l),
                              Continuous(λ_d))
           pv(deflator, cashflows)
       end;

julia> θ_base = [0.03, 0.012, 0.07, 0.001];   # i, μ, λ_l, λ_d

julia> base_pv = pv_of_components(θ_base...)
565.8746...

julia> ∇pv = ForwardDiff.gradient(θ -> pv_of_components(θ...), θ_base)
4-element Vector{Float64}:
 -2595.7788...   # ∂PV/∂i   (interest force)
 -2595.7788...   # ∂PV/∂μ   (mortality force)
 -2595.7788...   # ∂PV/∂λ_l (lapse force)
 -2595.7788...   # ∂PV/∂λ_d (default force)

The four sensitivities are equal in magnitude. That’s not a coincidence — it’s a structural feature of the independence-product. Under compose, each constant force enters the deflator the same way (factor = exp(-Σf · t)), so ∂PV/∂f_k = -Σ t·cᵗ·exp(-Σf·t) independently of which force f_k you differentiate. The risk decomposition tells you: “under this composition, a 1 bp shift in mortality moves the reserve by the same amount as a 1 bp shift in lapse, default, or interest.” Once you see it as a property of the composition algebra (not as a numerical coincidence), it’s the right answer.

Term-structure components break the symmetry because their factor isn’t a single exponential. Swap the constant interest force for a fitted yield curve — the same compose and the same ForwardDiff.gradient keep working, and the yield’s contribution now reflects curve shape:

julia> yc = fit(Spline.Cubic(), CMTYield.(rates, mats), Fit.Bootstrap());

julia> function pv_with_curve(μ, λ_l, λ_d)
           deflator = compose(yc, Continuous(μ), Continuous(λ_l), Continuous(λ_d))
           pv(deflator, cashflows)
       end;

julia> ∇ = ForwardDiff.gradient(θ -> pv_with_curve(θ...), [0.012, 0.07, 0.001])
3-element Vector{Float64}:
 -2427.2897...   # ∂PV/∂μ — symmetric across constant-force components
 -2427.2897...   # ∂PV/∂λ_l
 -2427.2897...   # ∂PV/∂λ_d

The decrement sensitivities are still mutually equal (constant forces composing symmetrically), but their magnitudes shift to reflect the yield curve’s shape. To get the yield curve’s per-tenor sensitivities — the analog of mortality/lapse/default per-component — use FinanceModels’s ZeroRateCurve and ActuaryUtilities’s KeyRates()/sensitivities, which already use ForwardDiff to differentiate against each tenor of the curve. Combining the two gives you a full risk decomposition (yield key rates × decrement forces) in a single AD sweep over the composed deflator.

Mortality, lapse, and credit sensitivities now live in the same toolkit as interest-rate sensitivities instead of requiring separate finite-difference plumbing per assumption.

Use case 4: Joint-life and last-survivor

A joint-and-survivor annuity pays while at least one of two lives is alive. A joint-life insurance pays on the first death. These two statuses sit on opposite sides of the inclusion-exclusion identity, and they show off two facets of the abstraction.

Joint-life status (both alive). Survival of the joint-life status under independence is S_xy(t) = S_x(t) · S_y(t). That’s literally what compose does:

julia> # Two constant-force lives (toy example; the structure extends to
julia> # any AbstractDeflator-based mortality)
julia> life_x = Continuous(0.012);   # 65-year-old, μ = 0.012

julia> life_y = Continuous(0.020);   # 70-year-old, μ = 0.020

julia> i      = Continuous(0.03);    # 3% interest

julia> # 10-year temporary joint-life annuity-due:
julia> # pays 1 at t=0, 1, ..., 9 while BOTH alive
julia> ä_xy_10 = pv(compose(i, life_x, life_y),
                    [Cashflow(1.0, t) for t in 0.0:9.0])
7.6859...

compose(i, life_x, life_y) is the joint-life deflator. No special-casing — the abstraction’s product structure is the independence assumption that joint-life pricing already uses.

Last-survivor status (at least one alive). Survival of the last-survivor status is 1 - F_xy(t) = S_x(t) + S_y(t) - S_x(t)·S_y(t) under independence. That’s not a product of marginals, so compose won’t help. We define a custom subtype:

struct LastSurvivor{A, B} <: AbstractDeflator
    a::A
    b::B
end

function FinanceCore.factor(ls::LastSurvivor, from, to)
    Sa = factor(ls.a, from, to)
    Sb = factor(ls.b, from, to)
    Sa + Sb - Sa * Sb     # P[at least one survives]
end
# pv goes through single-arg factor (factor(deflator, t)), so origin-invariant
# subtypes need to define it. For LastSurvivor with constant-force lives,
# "from time 0 to time t" is well-defined, so we delegate to the two-arg form:
FinanceCore.factor(ls::LastSurvivor, t) = factor(ls, zero(t), t)

# No `intensity` method — last-survivor force is non-trivial; let
# the subtype's user define it if needed.

The LastSurvivor is itself an AbstractDeflator, so it composes with the yield curve exactly like any other deflator:

julia> last_surv = LastSurvivor(life_x, life_y);

julia> # 10-year last-survivor annuity-due:
julia> ä_overline_xy_10 = pv(compose(i, last_surv),
                             [Cashflow(1.0, t) for t in 0.0:9.0])
8.7201...

The compose(i, last_surv) says “discount the last-survivor cashflow at the yield curve” — which is the right composition because the last-survivor status is jointly correlated, but its dependence on the yield is still independent. The type system makes the dependence structure visible.

This is the general pattern for correlated decrements: define a custom subtype whose factor encodes the correlation, then compose that subtype with anything else (yield, second-order decrements) under the standard independence assumption. Copulas, common-shock models, and joint analytical survival functions all fit this shape.

Use case 5: Parametric mortality models

MortalityTables.jl parametric models (Makeham, Gompertz, Thiele, Heligman-Pollard, etc.) all subtype AbstractMortality <: AbstractDeflator directly. Their factor semantic matches the existing survival(m, age) convention — survival from age 0 to a given age:

julia> using MortalityTables, FinanceCore

julia> mort = Makeham(a = 2.5e-5, b = 0.10, c = 1e-4);

julia> intensity(mort, 65)              # = hazard(mort, 65)
0.01672...

julia> factor(mort, 65, 70)             # 5-year survival from age 65
0.8973...

julia> factor(mort, 65)                 # survival from age 0 to age 65
0.8415...

To compose with a yield curve on a years-from-valuation axis, shift the parametric model’s age origin to the valuation age — the same at_age helper used for MortalityVector:

julia> mort_at_65 = at_age(mort, 65)
at_age(Makeham{Float64, Float64, Float64}(2.5e-5, 0.1, 0.0001), 65.0)

julia> factor(mort_at_65, 5)            # 5-year survival from age 65
0.8973...

julia> intensity(mort_at_65, 0)         # force at the valuation age
0.0167...

julia> deflator = compose(yc, mort_at_65);

julia> pv(deflator, cashflows)

at_age works on any AbstractMortality — parametric, vector-based, or any future subtype — so the same idiom drops a Makeham model, a select-ultimate table, or a custom Heligman-Pollard fit into the same composition workflow.

What’s not in this abstraction

Just as important as what fits is what doesn’t. The abstraction covers linear cashflow valuation under a deflator: pv(deflator, cashflows) = Σ cᵢ · factor(deflator, tᵢ). It does not cover:

  • Option pricing and contingent claims. A European call’s price isn’t factor × cashflow — it’s E[max(S(T) - K, 0)] · D(0,T) with the expectation taken over the whole distribution of S(T). Volatility matters; the deflator framework can’t see it. Option pricing lives in parallel present_value(model, contract) machinery (already in FinanceModels.jl); it’s orthogonal — a model can subtype AbstractDeflator for forward/discount calculations and implement present_value(_, ::Option) for option pricing.
  • Path-dependent payoffs, American exercise, lookbacks, basket payoffs that aren’t simple functions of marginal factors.
  • LDTI net premium ratio computation and other accounting-methodology-specific reserve mechanics. The deflator is an input to those calculations, not a replacement for them.

What this means in practice: a Variable Annuity GMxB liability has both a deflator side (yield × mortality × lapse for the actuarial scaffolding) and an option side (the guarantee payoff). The combined pricing flows through both abstractions; neither subsumes the other.

The four PRs

This release is a coordinated four-PR rollout:

  • FinanceCore.jl 2.6.0 — PR #39. Introduces AbstractDeflator, the required factor and optional intensity primitives, the compose constructor with two backends (tuple and vector), and the derived discount / accumulation / forward / Base.zero plumbing. Rate <: AbstractDeflator is the first concrete subtype.
  • FinanceModels.jl 5.4.0 — PR #255. AbstractYieldModel <: AbstractDeflator. Two-line factor plumbing routes through each yield model’s existing discount. Every concrete yield curve — Constant, Spline, MonotoneConvex, SmithWilson, NelsonSiegelSvensson, CairnsPritchard, ZeroRateCurve, plus the stochastic short-rate models Vasicek, CIR, and HullWhite — composes through compose(...).
  • MortalityTables.jl 3.0.0 — PR #134. A small deflator-aware hierarchy: AbstractMortality <: AbstractDeflator with three subtypes — MortalityVector (the new return type from UltimateMortality(...) and SelectMortality[issue_age], replacing OffsetArray while preserving the AbstractArray interface by delegation), ParametricMortality (Makeham, Gompertz, Heligman-Pollard, Thiele, etc.), and ShiftedMortality (constructed via at_age(model, age) — the idiomatic axis-alignment helper). Major version bump for the breaking type-tree change.
  • ActuaryUtilities.jl 5.6.0 — PR #133. Compat bumps; existing yield-specific machinery (duration, convexity, KeyRateDuration, sensitivities) stays unchanged. The pv re-export accepts composed deflators directly.

Each PR is non-breaking by itself. Adding <: AbstractDeflator is purely additive from upstream; the supertype change moves yield models out from under FinanceModels’s internal AbstractModel, which only affected one dispatch site (handled in the same PR).

Eight precedents this commits to

If you’re building on top of this abstraction, here’s what’s locked once the four PRs ship:

  1. Rate <: AbstractDeflator is permanent.
  2. factor(p, from, to) signature is locked: positional, no kwargs.
  3. intensity is optional with no fallback ever — adding numerical-differentiation later would break code that relies on MethodError as a discrete-vs-continuous guard.
  4. compose is the public composition primitive. * is not overloaded for deflator pairs (FinanceModels already overloads + for yield-spread addition and * for after-tax scaling; introducing a third meaning would have muddied two established algebras).
  5. (from, to) is the canonical time form; single-argument factor(p, t) is at subtype discretion (only origin-invariant or pre-aligned subtypes should define it).
  6. Independence is the composite default; correlated joints live in bespoke subtypes.
  7. Discrete-time deflators are first-class without traits — subtypes police their own time-argument validity at runtime.
  8. The name AbstractDeflator is locked; renaming is breaking.

Conclusion

The deflator abstraction is small — one abstract type, one required method, one optional method, one composition operator. The pay-off is conceptual unity: the same compose and pv calls work across yield, mortality, lapse, default, and (with custom subtypes) joint statuses, copulas, and stochastic scenarios. Code that used to live in four parallel APIs converges on one.

If you’re thinking about how this fits a specific actuarial problem you’re working on — variable annuity guarantees, defaultable insurance asset modeling, multi-life valuation, ESG-driven projections, anything else — the four PRs above are open for review and the abstraction is small enough to skim end-to-end in fifteen minutes. Feedback welcome.
